# RabbitEmergency.com

> Educational rabbit-emergency triage for owners across Asia: recognise urgent signs, reach a rabbit-savvy vet fast, and understand vet-guided recovery support. Not a substitute for veterinary care; products are recovery support used after a vet assesses the rabbit, never an emergency treatment.

## Emergency guides
- [Rabbit not eating or pooping](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-not-eating-or-pooping/): A rabbit that stops eating and passing droppings may be in gut stasis (GI stasis), which can become life-threatening within hours. Learn the warning signs and what to tell your vet.
- [Rabbit bloat / hard, tight belly](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-bloat-hard-belly/): A bloated, tight, or painful abdomen in a rabbit can signal gastric bloat or blockage, which is a true emergency. Learn the red flags and what to tell your vet immediately.
- [Rabbit collapsed in the heat](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-collapsed-in-heat/): Rabbits overheat easily and heatstroke is a life-threatening emergency. Learn the warning signs, safe first steps while you reach a vet, and what not to do.
- [Soft stool or no caecotrophs](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-soft-stool-no-caecotrophs/): Soft, mushy stool or uneaten caecotrophs stuck to a rabbit can signal diet imbalance, dental disease, or illness. Learn when it's urgent and what to tell your vet.
- [Head tilt in rabbits](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-head-tilt/): A sudden head tilt (torticollis) in a rabbit can be caused by inner-ear infection, E. cuniculi, or other conditions. Learn why it needs prompt veterinary care.
- [Flystrike in rabbits](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-flystrike/): Flystrike (myiasis) is a rapidly life-threatening emergency in rabbits. Learn how to recognise it, what to do immediately, and how to prevent it.
- [Rabbit not drinking / dehydrated](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-not-drinking-dehydrated/): A rabbit that stops drinking can dehydrate quickly, which worsens gut stasis and other illness. Learn the signs of dehydration and what to tell your vet.
- [Drooling or overgrown teeth](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-overgrown-teeth-drooling/): Drooling, a wet chin, dropping food, or weight loss in a rabbit often signals dental disease. Learn when it's urgent and what your vet may check.
- [Rabbit not eating after surgery](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-post-op-not-eating/): It's common for rabbits to eat less right after surgery or a vet visit, but a rabbit that won't eat or pass droppings post-op can slip into gut stasis. Learn the warning line.
- [Moulting and hair in the gut](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-moulting-hairball-gut/): Heavy moults mean rabbits swallow more fur, which can contribute to gut slowdown. Learn how to support gut movement and recognise when a slow gut becomes an emergency.
- [Rabbit difficulty breathing](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-difficulty-breathing/): Rabbits are obligate nasal breathers, so laboured or open-mouth breathing is always an emergency. Learn the red flags and what to tell your vet immediately.
- [Rabbit seizure](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-seizure/): A seizure in a rabbit is an emergency. Learn how to protect your rabbit, what to note for the vet, and the common causes including E. cuniculi and heatstroke.
- [Rabbit lethargic or not moving](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-lethargic-not-moving/): A rabbit that is suddenly very still, weak, or unresponsive is showing a serious warning sign. Learn what it can mean and what to tell your vet.
- [Blood in rabbit urine](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-blood-in-urine/): Red rabbit urine is often harmless plant pigment, but true blood can signal bladder, kidney, or reproductive disease. Learn how to tell the difference and when to see a vet.
- [Rabbit straining to urinate](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-straining-to-urinate/): A rabbit straining to urinate, dribbling, or unable to pass urine may have bladder stones, sludge, or a blockage. Learn when it's an emergency.
- [Rabbit eye injury or discharge](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-eye-injury-discharge/): A weepy, cloudy, swollen, or injured rabbit eye can signal infection, a scratch, dental disease, or a blocked tear duct. Learn when it's urgent.
- [Rabbit bleeding from a nail or wound](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-bleeding-nail-or-wound/): A broken nail or wound in a rabbit can bleed and become infected. Learn how to control bleeding safely and when a vet visit is needed.
- [Rabbit fell or was dropped](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-fall-or-trauma/): Rabbits have fragile spines and can injure themselves struggling or after a fall. Learn the warning signs of trauma and spinal injury and when to see a vet.
- [Rabbit weak or dragging back legs](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-hind-leg-weakness/): Sudden hind-leg weakness or paralysis in a rabbit can stem from spinal injury, E. cuniculi, or other causes. Learn why it needs prompt veterinary care.
- [Rabbit snuffles and runny nose](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-snuffles-runny-nose/): Sneezing, a runny nose, or 'snuffles' in a rabbit can signal an upper respiratory infection that needs veterinary care. Learn the warning signs.
- [Rabbit ear problems and scratching](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-ear-infection-scratching/): Persistent ear scratching, head shaking, crusts, or discharge can mean ear mites or infection in rabbits. Learn when it's urgent and what your vet may check.
- [Rabbit abscess or lump](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-abscess-or-lump/): A lump or abscess on a rabbit often needs veterinary treatment because rabbit pus is thick and abscesses recur. Learn the warning signs and what your vet may do.
- [Rabbit overgrown nails](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-overgrown-nails/): Overgrown rabbit nails can curl, snag, and break painfully. Learn the risks, when to see a vet, and how trimming is done safely.
- [Sore hocks in rabbits](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-sore-hocks/): Sore hocks (pododermatitis) are inflamed, sometimes ulcerated areas on the underside of a rabbit's feet. Learn the causes, warning signs, and when to see a vet.
- [Cold rabbit / hypothermia](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-cold-hypothermia/): A rabbit that is cold, weak, and unresponsive may be hypothermic, often alongside illness or gut stasis. Learn how to warm it safely and when it's an emergency.
- [Rabbit ate something toxic](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-ate-something-toxic/): If your rabbit ate a toxic plant, human food, or chemical, act fast. Learn common rabbit toxins, what to tell the vet, and why not to wait.
- [Rabbit choking or gagging](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-choking-or-gagging/): A rabbit pawing at its mouth, gagging, or struggling to breathe may have something stuck. Learn the warning signs and why it's an emergency.
- [Rabbit birth emergencies](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-giving-birth-emergency/): Most rabbits give birth easily, but a labouring doe in trouble is an emergency. Learn the warning signs of birthing problems and when to call a vet.
- [Baby rabbit not eating or orphaned](https://rabbitemergency.com/baby-rabbit-not-eating/): Sick or orphaned baby rabbits (kits) decline very fast. Learn the urgent signs, why you shouldn't feed the wrong milk, and when to get help now.
- [Overweight rabbit and diet](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-overweight-diet/): Obesity drives many rabbit health problems, from sore hocks to caecotroph issues and flystrike risk. Learn the right rabbit diet and when to involve your vet.
- [Safe and unsafe foods for rabbits](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-safe-unsafe-foods/): Knowing which foods are safe and which are dangerous helps prevent rabbit emergencies. Learn the basics and what to do if your rabbit eats something risky.
- [Recovery after gut stasis](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-recovery-after-gut-stasis/): After your vet treats gut stasis, careful feeding, hydration, and monitoring help your rabbit recover. Learn what to watch for and how to support the gut.
- [Recovery after surgery](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-recovery-after-surgery/): Good post-operative care helps rabbits recover from spay, neuter, or other surgery. Learn what's normal, what's a red flag, and how to support eating.
- [Syringe-feeding a rabbit](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-syringe-feeding-guide/): Syringe-feeding can keep a recovering rabbit's gut moving, but only on veterinary advice. Learn the basics, safe technique, and when to call your vet.
- [Stress in rabbits and illness](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-stress-and-illness/): Stress from heat, loud noise, travel, predators, or a lost companion can tip rabbits into not eating and gut stasis. Learn how to reduce stress and when to act.
- [Signs your rabbit is in pain](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-pain-signs/): Rabbits hide pain instinctively. Learn the subtle signs of pain in rabbits and why any sudden change in behaviour warrants a vet check.
- [Rabbit grinding teeth](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-grinding-teeth/): Soft tooth purring is contentment, but loud tooth grinding in a rabbit usually signals pain. Learn the difference and when to see a vet.
- [Watery diarrhoea in rabbits](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-watery-diarrhea/): Unlike soft caecotrophs, true watery diarrhoea in a rabbit is an emergency, especially in young rabbits. Learn the warning signs and what to do.

## Find a vet
- [Rabbit emergency vet in Hong Kong](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-emergency-vet-hong-kong/)
- [Rabbit emergency vet in Tokyo](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-emergency-vet-tokyo/)
- [Rabbit emergency vet in Taipei](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-emergency-vet-taipei/)
- [Rabbit emergency vet in Singapore](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-emergency-vet-singapore/)
- [Rabbit emergency vet in Bangkok](https://rabbitemergency.com/rabbit-emergency-vet-bangkok/)

## About & trust
- [About RabbitEmergency.com](https://rabbitemergency.com/about/): RabbitEmergency.com is an educational rabbit-emergency resource that helps owners recognise urgent signs, reach a rabbit-savvy vet, and understand vet-guided recovery support.
- [Editorial policy](https://rabbitemergency.com/editorial-policy/): How RabbitEmergency.com writes, reviews, and sources its rabbit-emergency guidance, and the claim discipline we apply to recovery products.
- [Medical disclaimer](https://rabbitemergency.com/medical-disclaimer/): RabbitEmergency.com provides general educational information only and is not a substitute for professional veterinary advice, diagnosis, or treatment.
- [How our guidance is reviewed](https://rabbitemergency.com/veterinary-review/): RabbitEmergency.com guidance is reviewed by an exotic veterinary advisory board against RWAF, House Rabbit Society, and exotic small-mammal medicine standards.
- [Clinical sources & standards](https://rabbitemergency.com/sources/): The veterinary and rabbit-welfare sources and standards behind RabbitEmergency.com guidance.
